name: Gayatri Mantra
english: We meditate on the divine light of the Sun; may it illuminate our intellect
original: ॐ भूर्भुवः स्वः तत्सवितुर्वरेण्यं भर्गो देवस्य धीमहि धियो यो नः प्रचोदयात्
transliteration: Om Bhur Bhuva Swaha, Tat Savitur Varenyam, Bhargo Devasya Dhimahi, Dhiyo Yo Nah Prachodayat
abstract:
The Gayatri Mantra is one of the oldest and most revered hymns in the Vedic tradition, originating in the Rigveda (Mandala 3, hymn 62, verse 10), composed by the sage Vishwamitra. It is addressed to Savitri, the solar deity who personifies the life-giving power of the sun. The mantra has been chanted for over three thousand years and is considered the mother of all Vedic mantras, embodying the essence of the Vedas in a single verse.

The three vyahritis — Bhur, Bhuva, and Swaha — represent the three planes of existence: the physical world, the astral or intermediate world, and the celestial world. Together they invoke the totality of creation and situate the practitioner within the cosmic order. This tripartite structure reflects the Vedic understanding that the universe is fundamentally threefold in nature.

The core of the mantra — Tat Savitur Varenyam Bhargo Devasya Dhimahi — is a meditation on the glorious light of the divine sun. The word Bhargo refers not merely to physical sunlight but to the supreme spiritual radiance that dispels ignorance and illuminates consciousness. The sun is understood here as a symbol of Brahman, the ultimate reality, whose light sustains all life and makes all perception possible.

The final line — Dhiyo Yo Nah Prachodayat — is a supplication: may that divine intelligence inspire and direct our intellects toward truth and righteousness. This prayer for right thinking and clarity of mind has made the Gayatri Mantra central to the Brahmacharya initiation ceremony (Upanayana), where it is traditionally whispered into the ear of a young student beginning Vedic studies. Daily recitation at sunrise, noon, and sunset — known as Sandhyavandana — has been a cornerstone of Hindu practice for millennia.

translations.en: Om. O Earth, Atmosphere, and Heaven. We meditate on the glorious splendour of the divine Sun. May that divine light illuminate our intellect and inspire us toward truth.
translations.zh: 嗡。大地、空气、天空。我们冥想神圣太阳的光辉。愿那神圣之光照耀我们的智慧，引导我们走向真理。
translations.es: Om. Oh Tierra, Atmósfera y Cielo. Meditamos en el esplendor glorioso del Sol divino. Que esa luz divina ilumine nuestro intelecto y nos inspire hacia la verdad.

name: Om Namah Shivaya
english: I bow to Shiva, the auspicious one who is the true self
abstract:
Om Namah Shivaya is among the most widely chanted mantras in the Shaiva tradition of Hinduism, appearing first in the Krishna Yajurveda's Shri Rudram. It is revered as the Panchakshara or five-syllable mantra, referring to the five sacred syllables Na-Ma-Shi-Va-Ya, each of which corresponds to one of the five elements that compose the universe and the body.

The five syllables map directly onto the Panchamahabhutas: Na corresponds to earth (Prithvi), Ma to water (Jala), Shi to fire (Agni), Va to air (Vayu), and Ya to ether or space (Akasha). By chanting this mantra, the practitioner is understood to purify and balance these five elements within themselves, restoring harmony between the individual body and the cosmic order.

The word Shiva itself means auspiciousness, and in the non-dual Shaiva tradition — particularly Kashmir Shaivism — Shiva is not merely a god but the universal consciousness that underlies all existence. The mantra is therefore not only an act of devotion to a deity but an affirmation of the practitioner's own deepest nature. Namah means both salutation and surrender; it implies the dissolution of ego as one bows to the truth of one's own being.

The mantra was popularized in the modern era by the Shaiva saint Thirugnana Sambandhar and is prominently featured in the Tamil Shaiva canon, the Thevaram. The sage Vasishtha declared it the greatest of all mantras in the Shiva Purana. Its six syllables including Om resonate across all sects of Hinduism, and it remains a living practice chanted during Shivaratri, daily puja, and meditative retreats worldwide.

translations.en: Om. I bow in reverence to the auspicious Lord Shiva, the supreme consciousness who dwells within as the true self.
translations.zh: 嗡。我向吉祥的湿婆神致敬，那存在于内心深处的至高意识，即真正的自我。
translations.es: Om. Me inclino con reverencia ante el auspicioso Señor Shiva, la conciencia suprema que mora en el interior como el verdadero ser.

name: Mahamrityunjaya Mantra
english: Great victory over death; liberate me from the bondage of mortality as a ripe fruit from its stem
original: ॐ त्र्यम्बकं यजामहे सुगन्धिं पुष्टिवर्धनम् उर्वारुकमिव बन्धनान् मृत्योर्मुक्षीय मामृतात्
transliteration: Om Tryambakam Yajamahe Sugandhim Pushtivardhanam, Urvarukamiva Bandhanan Mrityor Mukshiya Mamritat
abstract:
The Mahamrityunjaya Mantra, also called the Mritasanjivini Mantra or the Rudra Mantra, is one of the most potent and ancient Vedic hymns, appearing in the Rigveda (7.59.12) and the Yajurveda. It is attributed to the sage Markandeya, who according to legend received this mantra as a child and used it to overcome his destined early death, ultimately conquering Yama, the god of death, through the grace of Shiva.

The three eyes of Tryambaka represent the sun, the moon, and fire — or alternatively, the three states of consciousness: waking, dreaming, and deep sleep. The mantra praises Shiva as Sugandhim, the fragrant one whose divine fragrance pervades all of creation, and as Pushtivardhanam, the nourisher who sustains and increases the vitality of all beings.

The central prayer asks for liberation from the binding grip of death, compared poetically to a ripe cucumber or melon separating naturally from its vine. The image of the fruit and vine is deeply meaningful: just as a ripe fruit does not violently tear from its vine but detaches at the moment of perfect maturity, the mantra seeks a death that comes at the right time and brings spiritual liberation rather than continued rebirth.

Traditionally chanted 108 times during illness, crisis, or grief, the Mahamrityunjaya Mantra is used in Vedic funeral rites, healing ceremonies, and on the night of Shivaratri. It appears in the Shiva Purana as the supreme healer of all diseases — physical, mental, and spiritual. Modern practitioners use it as a meditation for confronting fear, releasing attachment, and reconnecting with the eternal dimension of consciousness that transcends birth and death.

translations.en: Om. We worship the three-eyed Lord Shiva who is fragrant and who nourishes all beings. May he liberate us from the bondage of death, as a ripe cucumber is severed from its vine, granting us immortality.
translations.zh: 嗡。我们崇拜三眼的湿婆神，他芬芳四溢，滋养一切众生。愿他将我们从死亡的束缚中解脱出来，如同成熟的黄瓜从藤蔓上被切断，赐予我们永恒。
translations.es: Om. Adoramos al Señor Shiva de tres ojos, que es fragante y que nutre a todos los seres. Que nos libere del cautiverio de la muerte, como un pepino maduro es cortado de su vid, otorgándonos la inmortalidad.

name: Om Namo Bhagavate Vasudevaya
english: Om, I bow to Lord Vasudeva (Krishna), the all-pervading divine being
abstract:
Om Namo Bhagavate Vasudevaya is the principal mantra of the Vaishnavite tradition and is classified as a Dvadasakshara or twelve-syllable mantra. It appears prominently in the Vishnu Purana, the Bhagavata Purana, and is associated with the Naradiya school of devotional practice. The mantra is a salutation to Vasudeva, a name for Krishna that means son of Vasudeva and also, etymologically, the one who dwells in all beings and in whom all beings dwell.

Bhagavan is a comprehensive term denoting the divine being who possesses all six supreme qualities in fullness: infinite knowledge, lordship, power, strength, virility, and splendour. The mantra is therefore not just a personal devotional address to Krishna but a recognition of the universal lordship of the divine in its most complete and perfect expression.

In the Bhagavata Purana, the sage Narada teaches this mantra to Dhruva, a young prince who seeks divine vision and ultimately attains liberation through unwavering devotion. The story of Dhruva is one of the most celebrated accounts of the mantra's efficacy: through its repetition in deep forest meditation, he achieves the pole-star station, a symbol of eternal, fixed consciousness.

The mantra is chanted in Vaishnava temples as part of the Pancharatra ritual system, during the recitation of the Vishnu Sahasranama, and in domestic puja. It forms the foundational practice of the Bhakti Yoga path as articulated in the Bhagavata Purana, where pure devotion without any ulterior motive is presented as the highest path to liberation.

translations.en: Om. I bow to the all-pervading Lord Vasudeva-Krishna, the supreme divine being who is the source and ground of all existence.
translations.zh: 嗡。我向无处不在的瓦苏德瓦-克里希纳神致敬，他是至高神圣的存在，是一切存在的源头和基础。
translations.es: Om. Me inclino ante el omnipresente Señor Vasudeva-Krishna, el ser divino supremo que es la fuente y el fundamento de toda existencia.

name: Om Shanti Shanti Shanti
english: Om, peace in body, peace in mind, peace in spirit
abstract:
The Shanti mantra, culminating in the threefold repetition of Shanti (peace), is one of the most universally recognized invocations in the Vedic and Upanishadic literature. The triple invocation of peace appears at the conclusion of major Upanishadic texts including the Brihadaranyaka, Chandogya, Taittiriya, and Mandukya Upanishads, where it serves as both an opening and closing benediction for study and practice.

The threefold repetition of Shanti is deeply intentional. In Vedic cosmology there are three types of obstacles or afflictions known as the Trividha Tapas: Adhibhautika (disturbances caused by other beings and the external world), Adhidaivika (disturbances caused by natural forces and fate), and Adhyatmika (disturbances arising from within the body and mind). The three invocations of Shanti petition for peace on all three of these levels simultaneously.

In the context of Vedic study, the Shanti Patha functions to create a sacred field of receptivity and protection around the act of learning. By invoking peace at the outset, the teacher and student acknowledge that true wisdom cannot be received in a disturbed or agitated state of mind. The mantra aligns the practitioner with the underlying peace that the Upanishads declare to be the very nature of Brahman.

In contemporary practice, Om Shanti Shanti Shanti is used as a closing invocation in yoga classes, meditation sessions, and prayer ceremonies. It is chanted at the beginning and end of pujas and is offered as a blessing at the conclusion of life-cycle rituals such as weddings and funerals. The mantra has transcended its Vedic origins to become a universal symbol of the aspiration for inner and outer peace.

translations.en: Om. Peace in the physical world, peace in the subtle and astral realms, peace in the spiritual sphere. May all afflictions cease and harmony prevail on every level of existence.
translations.zh: 嗡。物质世界中的平静，细微和星光界的平静，精神领域的平静。愿一切苦难消除，愿和谐在存在的每个层面上盛行。
translations.es: Om. Paz en el mundo físico, paz en los reinos sutiles y astrales, paz en la esfera espiritual. Que todas las aflicciones cesen y la armonía prevalezca en todos los niveles de la existencia.

name: Hare Krishna Maha Mantra
english: O Hari, O Krishna, O Rama — I call upon the divine energy and the supreme Lord
original: हरे कृष्ण हरे कृष्ण कृष्ण कृष्ण हरे हरे। हरे राम हरे राम राम राम हरे हरे।
transliteration: Hare Krishna Hare Krishna Krishna Krishna Hare Hare, Hare Rama Hare Rama Rama Rama Hare Hare
abstract:
The Hare Krishna Maha Mantra, composed of sixteen words arranged in a thirty-two syllable sequence, is the supreme mantra of the Gaudiya Vaishnava tradition founded by the saint Chaitanya Mahaprabhu in 16th-century Bengal. Chaitanya is believed by his followers to be a combined avatar of Radha and Krishna who descended to spread the path of devotional love and establish the chanting of the holy names as the primary spiritual practice for the current age, the Kali Yuga.

The three names — Hare, Krishna, and Rama — each carry profound significance. Hare is the vocative form of Hara, a name for Radha, the divine feminine energy or Shakti of the lord. Krishna means the all-attractive one, referring to the supreme personal deity who draws all beings toward himself through love. Rama is interpreted in this tradition as a name of Krishna meaning the source of transcendental pleasure.

Chaitanya and his successors systematized the theology of Bhakti around this mantra. The practice of chanting Hare Krishna on a set of 108-bead japa malas was prescribed as the central daily discipline. Advanced practitioners chant a minimum of sixteen rounds per day.

The mantra reached global prominence through A.C. Bhaktivedanta Swami Prabhupada, who founded ISKCON in 1966 and spread the practice throughout the Western world. The mantra's simple call-and-response structure makes it well-suited to congregational singing (Kirtan), and it was famously embraced by George Harrison of the Beatles.

translations.en: O divine energy of the Lord, O all-attractive Krishna, O source of all pleasure Rama — I offer my call and my heart to you.
translations.zh: 哦，主的神圣能量，哦，万物吸引的克里希纳，哦，一切快乐的源泉罗摩——我将我的呼唤和我的心献给你。
translations.es: Oh energía divina del Señor, oh Krishna el todo-atractivo, oh Rama fuente de todo placer — ofrezco mi llamado y mi corazón a ti.

name: Om Gam Ganapataye Namaha
english: Om, salutations to Ganesha, the remover of obstacles
abstract:
Om Gam Ganapataye Namaha is the primary mantra dedicated to Ganesha, the elephant-headed deity who is the lord of beginnings, the remover of obstacles (Vighnaharta), and the patron of arts, science, and wisdom. The mantra is drawn from the Ganapati Atharvasirsha, a late Upanishadic text of the Atharva Veda that presents Ganesha as the supreme reality underlying all existence. Ganesha is invariably invoked first in any Hindu ritual or undertaking, before all other deities.

The seed syllable Gam is the bija mantra of Ganesha — a single syllable encoding the entire energy and essence of the deity in its most concentrated form. In Tantric understanding, bija mantras are not symbolic references to a deity but actual sonic embodiments of their power. The syllable Gam is said to resonate at the frequency of the Muladhara chakra at the base of the spine, the energy center associated with earth, stability, grounding, and the removal of fear.

Ganapataye is a Sanskrit dative form meaning for Ganapati or to Ganapati. Ganapati is one of the principal names of Ganesha: Gana means a troop or assembly, and Pati means lord or master. Ganesha is thus the master of the ganas, and by extension the lord of all categories, groups, and principles of organization.

Ganesha worship has ancient Vedic roots and the festival of Ganesh Chaturthi, celebrated across India and particularly in Maharashtra, centers on this mantra. The mantra is chanted at the opening of business ventures, marriages, construction projects, and any new creative or spiritual endeavour.

translations.en: Om. I invoke the seed energy of Ganesha. Salutations to the lord of all beginnings and remover of obstacles, who grants wisdom, success, and auspicious starts.
translations.zh: 嗡。我召唤象神伽内什的种子能量。向一切开始的主宰和障碍的移除者致敬，他赐予智慧、成功和吉祥的开端。
translations.es: Om. Invoco la energía semilla de Ganesha. Salutaciones al señor de todos los comienzos y removedor de obstáculos, que otorga sabiduría, éxito y comienzos auspiciosos.

name: So Hum
english: I am That; I am the universal consciousness
abstract:
So Hum (also written Soham) is one of the most profound and intimate mantras of the Upanishadic and Yogic traditions. It is derived from the Sanskrit sentence Sa aham, meaning He am I or I am That, where That refers to the ultimate reality, Brahman. The mantra appears in the Isha Upanishad and the Hamsa Upanishad as a natural mantra synchronized with the breath.

The unique power of So Hum lies in its identification with the breath itself. In yogic physiology, the inhalation naturally produces the sound So, and the exhalation naturally produces the sound Hum. This means that every living being, simply by breathing, is unconsciously repeating So Hum approximately 21,600 times per day. The practice involves bringing conscious awareness to this unconscious affirmation.

In the tradition of Advaita Vedanta, So Hum is understood as a Mahavakya — a great saying that encapsulates the entire non-dual teaching. The individual self (Atman) is not separate from or subordinate to the universal consciousness (Brahman); they are one and the same reality. The repeated contemplation of So Hum is a direct practice of dissolving that illusion by returning again and again to the truth of unity.

In Kriya Yoga and Kashmir Shaivism, So Hum is associated with the Hamsa, the divine swan of Hindu mythology. The mantra is used in Pranayama practices, Trataka, and deep meditation as a vehicle for Pratyahara (withdrawal of the senses) and Samadhi (absorption in the self). Its simplicity and intimate correspondence with the natural breath make it accessible to practitioners at all levels.

translations.en: I am That. With every inhalation I affirm my identity with the universal self, and with every exhalation I release the illusion of separateness.
translations.zh: 我即那个。每次吸气时，我肯定自己与宇宙自我的同一性，每次呼气时，我释放分离的幻觉。
translations.es: Yo soy Eso. Con cada inhalación afirmo mi identidad con el ser universal, y con cada exhalación libero la ilusión de separación.

name: Aham Brahmasmi
english: I am Brahman; I am the ultimate reality
abstract:
Aham Brahmasmi — I am Brahman — is one of the four Mahavakyas or Great Sayings of the Upanishadic tradition, drawn from the Brihadaranyaka Upanishad (1.4.10) of the Shukla Yajurveda. It is attributed to the sage Vamadeva and is associated with the Advaita Vedanta school of non-dual philosophy as codified by Adi Shankaracharya in the 8th century CE.

In the Brihadaranyaka Upanishad, the declaration arises in the context of Prajapati's primordial meditation before creation. In the beginning there was only Brahman, the infinite self-aware consciousness. That consciousness, upon reflecting on itself, declared: Aham Brahmasmi — I am Brahman. For the practitioner, contemplating and internalizing this statement is an invitation to replicate that same act of recognition within their own consciousness.

The word Aham is the Sanskrit first-person pronoun, but in Advaita philosophy it points not to the limited ego-self but to the pure witness consciousness (Sakshi) that underlies all mental states. Brahman is derived from the root Brih, meaning to expand or to grow, and refers to the reality that is infinitely expansive, self-luminous, and the ground of all being.

In traditional Vedantic pedagogy, Aham Brahmasmi is not given as a mantra for repetition in the ordinary sense but as a Mahavakya for Manana (deep contemplation) and Nididhyasana (sustained meditative absorption). Its radical non-dualism has influenced modern spiritual teachers from Ramana Maharshi to contemporary Advaita teachers worldwide.

translations.en: I am Brahman. The individual self is not separate from the infinite universal consciousness; they are one and the same indivisible reality.
translations.zh: 我即梵。个体自我与无限的宇宙意识并非分离；它们是同一个不可分割的实在。
translations.es: Yo soy Brahman. El ser individual no está separado de la conciencia universal infinita; son una y la misma realidad indivisible.

name: Tat Tvam Asi
english: That thou art; you are the ultimate reality
abstract:
Tat Tvam Asi — That thou art — is perhaps the most philosophically celebrated of the four Mahavakyas, appearing nine times in the Chandogya Upanishad (6.8.7 through 6.16.3) of the Sama Veda in the dialogue between the sage Uddalaka Aruni and his son Shvetaketu. After Shvetaketu returns from twelve years of Vedic study proud of his learning, his father guides him through a series of vivid analogies to demonstrate that the underlying essence of all things is the one infinite being.

The philosophical unpacking of this three-word sentence has generated some of the richest commentary in the history of Indian thought. The word Tat (That) refers to Brahman, the absolute reality that transcends all particular forms and qualities. The word Tvam (Thou) refers to the individual self. The word Asi (art) asserts their identity.

Adi Shankaracharya's Advaita Vedanta interpretation holds that the apparent difference between Tvam and Tat is due to Upadhi or limiting adjuncts — just as space inside a pot appears different from space outside it, though both are the same space. The method of understanding Tat Tvam Asi requires setting aside the surface denotations of both Tat and Tvam and recognizing the shared ground of pure consciousness that they point to.

This single three-word mantra generated centuries of vigorous philosophical debate that shaped all subsequent Hindu theology. In Ramanujacharya's Vishishtadvaita and Madhvacharya's Dvaita, the same sentence is interpreted differently. As a meditative mantra, Tat Tvam Asi is used to dissolve the illusion of the separate ego by turning awareness toward the recognition of one's own identity with the ground of being.

translations.en: That thou art. The ultimate reality — the infinite consciousness that is the ground of all existence — is not other than what you are in your deepest nature.
translations.zh: 那即是你。终极实在——作为一切存在之基础的无限意识——与你最深层本性中的你并无不同。
translations.es: Eso eres tú. La realidad última — la conciencia infinita que es el fundamento de toda existencia — no es diferente de lo que eres en tu naturaleza más profunda.

name: Om Purnamadah Purnamidam
english: That is whole, this is whole; from wholeness comes wholeness, and wholeness remains
original: ॐ पूर्णमदः पूर्णमिदम् पूर्णात्पूर्णमुदच्यते। पूर्णस्य पूर्णमादाय पूर्णमेवावशिष्यते।
transliteration: Om Purnamadah Purnamidam Purnat Purnamudachyate, Purnasya Purnamadaya Purnamevavashishyate
abstract:
The Purnamadah Mantra is the Shanti Patha of both the Brihadaranyaka Upanishad and the Isha Upanishad, and it stands as one of the most mathematically and spiritually elegant statements in all of Vedic literature. The mantra encapsulates the Upanishadic teaching on the infinite nature of Brahman by asserting that infinity subtracted from infinity still leaves infinity.

The word Purna is derived from the root Prna meaning to fill completely, and it is often translated as whole, full, or complete. In the Upanishadic context, however, Purna is not merely a relative completeness — it is the absolute fullness of Brahman that cannot be diminished, increased, or affected in any way. Adah (That) is Purna. Idam (This, referring to the manifest universe) is also Purna.

The second line explains the logical consequence: from Purna (the whole, the absolute), Purna (the manifest world) emerges. The universe is not a diminishment or a fragment of Brahman; it is the whole expressing itself as the whole. This is the teaching of Vivartavada — like the ocean and its waves, where the ocean does not become less oceanic by producing waves.

The third and final line is the most paradoxical: if you take Purna from Purna, what remains is still Purna. The mantra is used as a morning invocation, a pre-study blessing, and a meditation on the nature of abundance and wholeness. Contemplating it helps dissolve the sense of lack, incompleteness, or fragmentation that underlies much human suffering.

translations.en: Om. That infinite reality is whole. This manifest universe is whole. From that wholeness, this wholeness comes forth. Even when wholeness is taken from wholeness, wholeness alone remains.
translations.zh: 嗡。那无限的实在是完整的。这个显现的宇宙也是完整的。从那完整中，涌现出这个完整。即使从完整中取走完整，留下的仍然是完整。
translations.es: Om. Esa realidad infinita es completa. Este universo manifiesto es completo. De esa plenitud surge esta plenitud. Incluso cuando se toma la plenitud de la plenitud, solo queda la plenitud.

name: Om Namah Shivaya Gurave
english: Om, I bow to Shiva the divine teacher who is sat-chit-ananda
original: ॐ नमः शिवाय गुरवे सच्चिदानन्दमूर्तये। निष्प्रपञ्चाय शान्ताय श्रीनिधये नमो नमः।
transliteration: Om Namah Shivaya Gurave Sacchidananda Murtaye, Nishprapanchaya Shantaya Shrinidhaye Namo Namah
abstract:
Om Namah Shivaya Gurave is the invocation mantra of the Anusara Yoga tradition and a deeply revered hymn in the broader Shaiva Tantra lineage. It presents the divine teacher (Guru) as an embodiment of the three qualities of Brahman — Sat (being), Chit (consciousness), and Ananda (bliss) — which together describe the essential nature of ultimate reality according to Advaita and Shaiva philosophy.

The word Gurave (to the Guru or teacher) is key to understanding this mantra's distinctive contribution to the Shaiva devotional canon. In the Guru-disciple tradition that runs through all schools of Indian philosophy, the Guru is not merely a human instructor but a living transmission of the divine. The Kularnava Tantra, the Guru Gita, and the Shiva Sutras all affirm that the Guru is Shiva in human form.

Nishprapanchaya means beyond the phenomenal world or transcendent of all manifestation. Shantaya means the peaceful, the tranquil, pointing to Shiva's quality of supreme equanimity. Shrinidhaye means the abode or treasure house of Shri — the divine beauty, grace, and auspiciousness that radiates from the awakened being. The concluding Namo Namah is a double salutation expressing profound reverence and complete self-surrender.

The mantra is traditionally chanted three times at the beginning of a Shaiva or Anusara yoga practice as an act of orientation and dedication. It serves to remind the practitioner that the ultimate purpose of all practice is the recognition of the divine grace that is already present within.

translations.en: Om. I bow to Shiva who appears as the Guru, whose form is being-consciousness-bliss, who is beyond the phenomenal world, who is utterly peaceful, and who is the abode of divine beauty and grace.
translations.zh: 嗡。我向以上师形象显现的湿婆神致敬，他的形态是存在-意识-喜乐，他超越现象世界，他完全安宁，他是神圣美丽与恩典的住所。
translations.es: Om. Me inclino ante Shiva que aparece como el Guru, cuya forma es ser-conciencia-bienaventuranza, que está más allá del mundo fenoménico, que es completamente pacífico, y que es la morada de la belleza y la gracia divinas.

name: Om
english: The primordial sound — the vibration from which all creation arises
abstract:
Om is the most fundamental of all mantras — the primordial sound that the Vedic tradition identifies as the vibrational substrate of all existence. Before any other mantra, any other prayer, any other sacred act, there is Om. It is described in the Mandukya Upanishad as comprising the entirety of time: A (the waking state), U (the dream state), M (the deep sleep state), and the silence that follows — representing turiya, the fourth transcendent state that underlies and encompasses the other three.

The cosmological significance of Om extends across every school of Hindu philosophy. In the Yoga tradition, Om is the Pranava — the vibration that both creates and sustains the universe. The Taittiriya Upanishad opens with Om as the bridge between the finite and the infinite: 'Om — this syllable is all this.' In Vedanta, Om is equated with Brahman — the absolute reality — and its three letters with the three aspects of the divine (Brahma/creation, Vishnu/preservation, Shiva/dissolution).

The physical and physiological effects of chanting Om have been extensively documented. The sustained vowel sounds resonate through the chest, throat, and skull, inducing a relaxation response in the autonomic nervous system. Research at the National Brain Research Centre in India found that Om chanting produces measurable changes in brain wave activity, particularly in the theta range associated with deep meditative states. The concluding nasal hum (the 'm') produces vibration in the frontal sinuses and palate associated with vagal nerve stimulation.

As a practice, Om is used both as a standalone meditation and as the opening and closing of other mantras and prayers. Simply sitting and sustaining the sound of Om — feeling the A in the belly, the U in the chest and throat, the M in the skull — for several minutes constitutes a complete and profound meditation. The silence at the end of each Om is as important as the sound itself; teachers say that the silence is where Om's deepest gift lives.

In contemporary yoga and meditation practice worldwide, Om has become possibly the most recognized spiritual sound on earth. Its universality speaks to something fundamental in the human nervous system's relationship to sustained vocal resonance. Countless practitioners from widely different spiritual backgrounds describe the same experience: a few rounds of Om, chanted slowly and mindfully, produce an immediate, unmistakable shift in consciousness toward stillness and openness.

translations.en: The primordial sound of the universe
translations.zh: 宇宙的原初之声
translations.es: El sonido primordial del universo

name: Om Saha Naavavatu
english: May we be protected together; may we be nourished together; may we work with great vigor together
original: ॐ सह नाववतु । सह नौ भुनक्तु । सह वीर्यं करवावहै । तेजस्वि नावधीतमस्तु मा विद्विषावहै । ॐ शान्तिः शान्तिः शान्तिः ॥
transliteration: Om Saha Naavavatu, Saha Nau Bhunaktu, Saha Veeryam Karavaavahai, Tejasvi Naavadheetamastu Maa Vidvishaavahai, Om Shantih Shantih Shantih
abstract:
Om Saha Naavavatu is the peace invocation (shanti patha) of the Taittiriya Upanishad and the Katha Upanishad, traditionally recited before a period of Vedic study to consecrate the relationship between teacher and student. Unlike the simple three-fold peace chant, this prayer is explicitly relational — it prays for the protection, nourishment, and vigorous effort of both teacher and student together, acknowledging that genuine learning is a shared endeavor that requires divine blessing on both parties.

The word 'saha' (together/jointly) appears three times in the opening three lines, emphasizing the communal nature of Vedic transmission. Knowledge in the Vedic tradition is not merely information transferred from a knower to a student but a living current that flows between two people whose relationship has been properly consecrated. The shanti patha creates this consecration — it invokes divine protection around the space of learning and around the relationship itself.

The petition for 'tejasvi' — brilliance or radiance — in the study ('may our study be luminous') reflects the Vedic understanding that sacred learning has a quality distinct from intellectual acquisition. When both teacher and student are properly prepared and the learning space is properly consecrated, the texts come alive in a way they cannot when approached merely as information. The luminosity invoked is the quality of realization rather than mere comprehension.

The final prayer 'Ma vidvishaavahai' — may we not dispute or quarrel with one another — is a built-in protection against the ego dynamics that often arise in teacher-student relationships: the student's resistance to being challenged, the teacher's defensiveness when questioned, the misunderstanding born of different levels of comprehension. By beginning with this prayer, both parties acknowledge the human tendency toward conflict and ask for divine help in transcending it.

The three-fold repetition of Shantih at the close invokes peace on three levels: the physical (Adhibhautika), the divine/supernatural (Adhidaivika), and the internal/mental (Adhyatmika). Any significant undertaking faces potential obstacles from all three levels, and the triple peace invocation addresses all three simultaneously, establishing the study session within a field of comprehensive divine protection.

translations.en: May we be protected and nourished together; may our study be luminous; may we not dispute with one another; Om Peace, Peace, Peace
translations.zh: 愿我们共同得到保护和滋养；愿我们的学习光辉灿烂；愿我们互不争论；嗡，平静，平静，平静
translations.es: Que seamos protegidos y nutridos juntos; que nuestro estudio sea luminoso; que no disputemos entre nosotros; Om Paz, Paz, Paz

name: Durga Gayatri Mantra
english: We meditate on the Goddess Durga; may she who protects all illuminate our minds
original: ॐ महादेव्यै च विद्महे दुर्गायै च धीमहि तन्नो देवी प्रचोदयात्
transliteration: Aum Mahaa-Dhevyai Cha Vidmahe Durgaayai Cha Dhimahee Tanno Dhevee Prachodayath
abstract:
The Durga Gayatri Mantra follows the meter and structure of the Vedic Gayatri Mantra but addresses Goddess Durga, the supreme warrior-goddess who destroys demonic forces and protects the cosmos. 'Mahadevyai' (Great Goddess) and 'Durgayai' (Durga — the inaccessible or invincible one) together describe the two aspects of divine feminine power: her transcendent omnipotence and her specific manifestation as the destroyer of what obstructs spiritual growth.

In the Devi Mahatmya (Durga Saptashati), Goddess Durga is described as the primordial energy (Adishakti) from whom all creation emerges and into whom it ultimately returns. She is not merely a female deity in a pantheon but the very power (shakti) that animates all existence — without her energy, even Brahma, Vishnu, and Shiva could not function. The Durga Gayatri invokes this cosmic power to illuminate the practitioner's intellect and inspire their actions.

The specific quality invoked by Durga's name — the inaccessibility of liberation and the difficulty of the spiritual path — makes the Durga Gayatri particularly potent for practitioners facing significant inner or outer obstacles. Where the Vedic Gayatri invokes the general divine light of the sun, the Durga Gayatri invokes the specific energy of the divine mother who cuts through obstacles with her sword of discrimination. The mantra is thus an invocation of clarifying, obstacle-destroying wisdom.

Navratri (the nine nights of the Goddess) is the primary occasion for intensive Durga Gayatri practice, with practitioners typically reciting it 108 times per day during the nine-day festival. The nine forms of the Goddess (Navadurga) are each invoked during specific nights, and the Durga Gayatri serves as the common thread connecting all nine nights of celebration. Its daily use throughout the year maintains a continuous connection with the protective and transformative energy of the divine mother.

For practitioners encountering significant challenges in their spiritual practice, worldly life, or psychological healing, the Durga Gayatri is prescribed as a specific antidote. The Goddess Durga is said to respond to sincere invocation with both the strength to face what must be faced and the wisdom to recognize when one's obstacles are internal — projections of fear, doubt, or accumulated karma — requiring the sword of inner discernment rather than external effort.

translations.en: We meditate on the Great Goddess Durga; may that divine Goddess illuminate our minds
translations.zh: 我们冥想大女神杜尔迦；愿那神圣女神照耀我们的心智
translations.es: Meditamos en la Gran Diosa Durga; que esa divina Diosa ilumine nuestra mente

name: Lokah Samastah Sukhino Bhavantu
english: May all beings everywhere be happy and free
abstract:
Lokah Samastah Sukhino Bhavantu — May all beings in all worlds be happy and free — is an ancient Sanskrit prayer from the Yoga and Vedic traditions, widely used as a closing prayer at the end of yoga practice and as a mantra of universal compassion. It expresses the highest aspiration of the spiritual life: not merely personal liberation or happiness, but the wellbeing of all sentient beings without exception or exclusion.

The phrase 'lokah samastah' encompasses all of creation — 'loka' can mean realm, world, or dimension, and 'samastah' means all or every. The prayer is thus strikingly comprehensive: not only all beings on earth, not only all humans, but all beings in all realms and dimensions. This universality reflects the Sanskrit understanding that the scope of compassion must be as vast as the scope of suffering — which recognizes no boundary of species, planet, or dimension.

The word 'sukhino' (happy/at ease) derives from the same root as 'sukha' — a pleasant axle-hole (su-kha), meaning a wheel that turns smoothly. Sukha is thus not merely emotional pleasure but the deep ease of a life in which things move without obstruction, where resistance has been released and natural flourishing prevails. 'Bhavantu' (may they be) is an optative form expressing heartfelt wish rather than confident prediction.

In yoga practice, Lokah Samastah is traditionally recited three times as a dedication at the end of practice. This act of dedication — offering the merit of the practice for the benefit of all beings rather than keeping it for oneself — is considered a fundamental spiritual act that prevents the ego from monopolizing the fruits of practice. By dedicating their effort to all beings, the practitioner expresses their understanding that individual liberation is inseparable from universal liberation.

The prayer is also widely used in secular contexts as an expression of universal goodwill. Its simple, inclusive scope makes it accessible to practitioners of any background: whether understood as a prayer to a personal God, a dedication of karmic merit, or simply a statement of intention, it reliably opens the heart beyond the narrow concerns of personal welfare and orients consciousness toward the larger good. Regular practice of this prayer is reported to gradually but genuinely expand one's circle of concern beyond family and community to encompass all of life.

translations.en: May all beings everywhere be happy and free
translations.zh: 愿一切众生皆幸福自由
translations.es: Que todos los seres en todas partes sean felices y libres

name: Om Shree Dhanvantre Namaha
english: Salutation to Lord Dhanvantari, divine physician of the gods
abstract:
Om Shree Dhanvantre Namaha is the primary mantra of Lord Dhanvantari, the divine physician who emerged from the cosmic ocean during the churning of the primordial ocean (Samudra Manthan) bearing the pot of amrita (nectar of immortality) and the Ayurvedic wisdom for healing all disease. As the deity of Ayurveda, Dhanvantari is considered the ultimate source of all healing knowledge and the presiding deity of the medical arts in Hindu tradition.

Dhanvantari is depicted as holding a pot of amrita in one hand and Ayurvedic herbs in another, with a conch shell and the discus of Vishnu (he is considered an avatar of Vishnu). His emergence from the ocean of milk represents the discovery that healing knowledge — like amrita itself — is a treasure hidden within the depths of existence, available to those who churn deeply enough. His mantra is an invocation of that healing intelligence, asking it to arise and work through the practitioner.

In traditional Ayurvedic practice, this mantra is recited before the preparation and administration of medicines, before medical consultations, and at the beginning of any healing endeavor. It transforms the act of healing from a purely technical intervention into a sacred act — aligning the healer's intention with the cosmic healing intelligence embodied by Dhanvantari. The mantra acknowledges that the healer is a vessel for a wisdom that transcends personal training.

The festival of Dhanteras — two days before Diwali — is specifically dedicated to Lord Dhanvantari. On this day, practitioners clean and light their homes as an invocation of health and prosperity, recite the Dhanvantari mantra, and make offerings to request health and vitality for the coming year. The mantra is considered particularly potent on this day, when Dhanvantari's energy is said to be most accessible.

For personal use, Om Shree Dhanvantre Namaha is recited during illness, before taking medicines, before medical procedures, or as a daily health protection practice. Teachers recommend reciting it 108 times while visualizing golden, healing light filling the body. Whether or not one accepts the literal existence of a healing deity, the practice of dedicating one's health to a larger intelligence — and approaching one's body with reverence rather than manipulation — is itself a healing act that contemporary mind-body medicine increasingly validates.

translations.en: Salutation to Lord Dhanvantari, the divine healer
translations.zh: 礼敬檀梵陀利主，神圣的疗愈者
translations.es: Salutación al Señor Dhanvantari, el sanador divino

name: Om Hreem Shreem Kleem Namaha
english: Invocation of divine manifestation, abundance, and desire through the seed syllables
abstract:
Om Hreem Shreem Kleem Namaha is a powerful Tantric mantra combining three of the most important bija (seed) syllables in the Shakta tradition. Each bija syllable is considered a concentrated vibrational encoding of a specific divine energy: Om is the universal sound of Brahman; Hreem is the bija of Maya and divine manifestation, associated with Bhuvanesvari and the creative power of the cosmic mother; Shreem is the bija of Lakshmi, the goddess of prosperity, abundance, and auspiciousness; and Kleem is the bija of Kama and attraction, associated with Krishna and the magnetic power of love and desire.

In Tantric theory, bija mantras are the vibrational seeds from which the full energies of deities grow. They are not descriptions of divine qualities but direct encodings of those qualities in sound. Chanting Hreem is, in this understanding, not merely calling on the divine mother but actually resonating with the specific frequency of her creative power. The combination of Hreem, Shreem, and Kleem therefore invokes three complementary dimensions of the divine feminine: the power to create and manifest (Hreem), the power to attract abundance and beauty (Shreem), and the power of love and magnetic attraction (Kleem).

The mantra is used in multiple Tantric traditions for different purposes. As a prosperity practice, the emphasis is on Shreem — the Lakshmi energy that attracts material and spiritual abundance. As a manifestation practice, the emphasis is on Hreem — the creative power that brings intentions into form. As a devotional or relationship practice, the emphasis is on Kleem — the magnetic power that draws divine grace and human connection. The combined mantra addresses all three simultaneously.

Traditional teachers prescribe initiatory guidance for working with Tantric bija mantras, as their energies are considered powerful and specific. Nevertheless, the mantra has been widely transmitted in accessible yoga and meditation contexts and is used by practitioners worldwide. Many report that regular practice produces increased synchronicity — a sense of life becoming more responsive to intention — alongside a more tangible experience of abundance consciousness.

For meditation, Om Hreem Shreem Kleem Namaha is typically recited 108 times with a mala, with each repetition accompanied by a specific intention held lightly in awareness. The practice is often combined with visualization of the golden light of Lakshmi or the brilliant white light of the cosmic mother. Teachers emphasize that the mantra's power depends not on the visualization but on the quality of attention — sincere, present, and free from desperate craving.

translations.en: Om, divine manifestation, abundance, desire — I bow
translations.zh: 嗡，神圣显化，丰盛，愿望——礼敬
translations.es: Om, manifestación divina, abundancia, deseo — me inclino

name: Jehi Vidhi Hoi Naath Hit Moraa
english: O Lord, do whatever is for my highest good — I am Your servant
original: जेहि विधि होई नाथ हित मोरा। करहु सो वेगि दास मैं तोरा॥
transliteration: Jehi Vidhi Hoi Naath Hit Moraa, Karahu So Vegi Daas Main Toraa
abstract:
Jehi Vidhi Hoi Naath Hit Moraa — O Lord, do whatever is for my good, do it quickly, for I am your servant — is a verse from the Ramcharitmanas, the devotional retelling of the Ramayana composed by the sixteenth-century saint-poet Goswami Tulsidas. It is one of the most beloved and widely recited couplets in the bhakti (devotional) tradition of northern India, expressing complete surrender of personal will to the divine, combined with the intimate, personal relationship characteristic of Vaishnava devotion.

The verse is placed in the context of a devotee's prayer for divine guidance and intervention, and its power lies in its combination of total surrender ('do whatever is for my good — not what I want, but what is truly beneficial') and urgency ('do it quickly'). This urgency is not impatience toward God but the devotee's recognition of their own helplessness — they know they cannot figure out what is best for themselves, and they ask the Lord not to delay in acting on their behalf.

The identification as 'daas' (servant) is significant. The path of daasya bhakti — devotion through the consciousness of being God's servant — is one of the five principal modes of devotional relationship in Vaishnavism. In this mode, the devotee experiences themselves not as an autonomous agent pursuing their own goals with divine assistance, but as an instrument of the divine will, whose very identity is defined by service to the Lord. This complete dissolution of self-determination into divine service is, paradoxically, described as the highest freedom.

Tulsidas himself was a devotee of Lord Rama, and the Ramcharitmanas is saturated with the mood of this surrender. He writes from direct experience of what it means to release one's cleverness and plans and simply ask the Lord to act. The verse encodes a specific psychological movement: from anxious strategizing ('what should I do?') to open-hearted surrender ('You know best — whatever You will'). This movement, consistently practiced, is what Tulsidas found to be the most reliable path to both inner peace and divine assistance.

As a mantra for daily life, Jehi Vidhi Hoi Naath is particularly valuable at decision points, crossroads, and moments of confusion. Rather than forcing clarity through extended deliberation, the practitioner pauses and offers this verse — acknowledging both their desire for the situation to resolve well and their recognition that the divine perspective transcends their limited understanding. Many practitioners report that this practice regularly produces unexpected solutions and openings that could not have been reached through personal cleverness alone.

translations.en: O Lord, do whatever is for my highest good — quickly — for I am Your servant
translations.zh: 主啊，为我做最有益的事——快快地——因为我是祢的仆人
translations.es: Señor, haz lo que sea para mi mayor bien — rápidamente — porque soy Tu siervo

name: Om Sarveshaam Svastir Bhavatu
english: May there be prosperity, peace, fullness, and auspiciousness in all things
original: ॐ सर्वेशां स्वस्तिर्भवतु । सर्वेशां शान्तिर्भवतु । सर्वेशां पूर्णम्भवतु । सर्वेशां मङ्गलम्भवतु ।
transliteration: Om Sarveshaam Svastir Bhavatu, Sarveshaam Shaantir Bhavatu, Sarveshaam Purnam Bhavatu, Sarveshaam Mangalam Bhavatu, Om Shaanti Shaanti Shaantih
abstract:
Om Sarveshaam Svastir Bhavatu is a Vedic peace prayer invoking welfare (svasti), peace (shanti), fullness (purnam), and auspiciousness (mangalam) for all beings — 'sarveshaam' literally means 'of all' or 'for all.' Like the Lokah Samastah prayer, this mantra expands the circle of blessing beyond the personal to encompass all of existence, but does so through four specific qualities that together constitute a comprehensive vision of flourishing life.

Each of the four qualities deserves contemplation: 'Svasti' (well-being/prosperity) comes from 'su' (good/well) and 'asti' (it is) — literally 'may it be well.' It is the same root as the ancient swastika symbol, before its misappropriation, which represented the auspicious turning of the cosmos. 'Shanti' (peace) is the deep, abiding stillness that underlies all activity. 'Purnam' (fullness/completeness) invokes the state described in the Isha Upanishad's great declaration: that completeness taken from completeness leaves completeness unchanged. 'Mangalam' (auspiciousness/blessing) is the quality that makes beginnings promising and transitions smooth.

The prayer is structured as four parallel petitions, each beginning with 'Sarveshaam' (for all), creating a rhythmic wave of blessing. This parallel structure is characteristic of Vedic prayer composition, where repetition with variation is used to deepen both the meaning and the vibratory impact of the words. The fourfold request also mirrors the Vedic understanding that complete welfare requires all four of these qualities — prosperity without peace is exhausting, peace without fullness is resignation, fullness without auspiciousness lacks joy.

In temple worship and family ceremonies, Om Sarveshaam Svastir Bhavatu is recited as a concluding blessing that extends the merit of any ritual outward to encompass all beings. This act of expansion prevents ritual from becoming self-serving and maintains its connection to the Vedic ideal of yajna — sacred action performed for the welfare of all. When individual practice ends with this prayer, the practitioner participates in the ancient tradition of offering their spiritual work as a gift to the world.

As a personal daily practice, reciting Om Sarveshaam Svastir Bhavatu first thing in the morning sets a tone of abundance-consciousness and compassion for the day. The prayer's four qualities — welfare, peace, fullness, and auspiciousness — become intentions that subtly shape how the practitioner moves through their day: with a wish for these qualities in every interaction, the quality of attention itself shifts from self-protection to blessing.

translations.en: May there be prosperity, peace, fullness, and auspiciousness for all beings
translations.zh: 愿一切众生皆有福祉、平静、圆满与吉祥
translations.es: Que haya prosperidad, paz, plenitud y buena fortuna para todos los seres

name: Lalitha Sahasranama
english: A thousand names of the Goddess Lalitha — the supreme divine mother
abstract:
Lalitha Sahasranama — the Thousand Names of Lalitha — is one of the most sacred texts in the Shakta tradition of Hinduism, found in the Brahmanda Purana in the form of a dialogue between the sage Hayagreeva (the horse-headed avatar of Vishnu, considered the repository of all wisdom) and the sage Agastya. The text consists of 1000 divine names of the Goddess Lalitha Tripura Sundari, each name a complete description of an aspect of the supreme divine feminine. Reciting all 1000 names is considered one of the most powerful acts of Shakta devotion.

Lalitha (meaning 'she who plays' or 'she who is graceful and easy') is one of the names of the goddess Tripura Sundari — 'the beautiful one of the three cities,' representing the integration of all three worlds. She is the highest form of the Goddess in the Sri Vidya tradition of Shakta Tantra, understood as the supreme reality itself in its feminine aspect. The Sahasranama presents her from every possible angle — as creator, sustainer, and destroyer; as the physical universe and as pure consciousness; as the most transcendent and the most intimately near.

The 1000 names are structured in three sections: poorva bhaga (the introduction, establishing the context and the authority of the transmission), the stotra itself (the 1000 names), and uttara bhaga (the conclusion, containing the phala shruti — the benefits of recitation). The stotra moves from descriptions of the Goddess's physical form (beginning with her feet and ascending to her crown) through her cosmological functions, her relationship to the Sri Chakra (the geometric yantra that is her visual form), and her identity with ultimate reality.

Reciting the Lalitha Sahasranama in full — traditionally done on Fridays, during Navratri, or on the practitioner's birthday — is a profound meditative practice that takes approximately 30-45 minutes and encompasses virtually the entire landscape of Shakta theology. Each name is a doorway into a specific aspect of the Goddess's reality. Practitioners describe the cumulative effect as a process of being gradually revealed to: as the names accumulate, the Goddess becomes more real, more present, more specific.

For practitioners who cannot recite all 1000 names, the practice of reciting the first name 'Shri Mata' (the blessed mother) and the last name 'Lalitambika' (the gentle mother) is considered the essence of the entire sahasranama — beginning from recognition of the divine mother's care and ending in her gentle, playful grace. The tradition is living and accessible; even brief contact with the Sahasranama's names is said to purify accumulated karma and orient the soul toward its highest nature.

translations.en: The thousand names of the Goddess Lalitha, the supreme divine mother
translations.zh: 女神拉利塔的千种名号——至高的神圣母亲
translations.es: Los mil nombres de la Diosa Lalitha, la suprema madre divina
